resume:
Senior User Researcher | Mixed-methods, fintech & health

Profile: Senior researcher with 9 years owning end-to-end studies that change product direction. I scope the question, choose the method, run the work, and drive decisions with stakeholders.

Experience

NorthGate Money — Senior User Researcher (2021–present)
- Situation: The lending team's drop-off at income verification was high but nobody knew why.
- Task: I was asked to find the root cause and recommend a fix within one quarter.
- Action: I ran a 14-participant moderated study plus an analysis of 30 days of funnel analytics, segmented by verification method, and co-ran two design sprints.
- Result: Identified that manual document upload caused most abandonment; the redesign cut verification drop-off from 38% to 22% in the following quarter.
- Built and maintained a research repository adopted by 4 product teams.

Vireo Health Labs — UX Researcher (2018–2021)
- Ran longitudinal diary studies and usability testing for a patient-adherence app
- Partnered with data science to triangulate qualitative findings against behavioral logs

Education: MS, Human-Computer Interaction
Methods: moderated/unmoderated usability, diary studies, surveys, funnel analysis, concept testing
jd: We are hiring a Senior User Researcher to embed with our payments product group and own high-stakes, decision-driving research. You will scope ambiguous questions, choose the right mixed-methods approach, run studies end-to-end, and turn findings into product decisions that leadership acts on. Responsibilities: lead 6–8 substantial studies per year, mentor two junior researchers, and own a research repository. We expect senior researchers to speak precisely about method choices and impact: why a given method fit the question, what the sample and design were, and what specifically changed as a result. In this conversation we'll go deep on one or two studies rather than survey your whole history, so come ready to walk through a study from question to decision, including the tradeoffs you weighed in the design.

history.q: Pick the study you're proudest of and give me the question you were trying to answer.
Walk me through how you designed it — method, sample, and why those fit the question.
What changed as a result, and how did you know the change was attributable to your research?

candidate_last_answer: The income-verification drop-off study at NorthGate. Situation: our lending funnel was bleeding users at the verification step — drop-off was 38% there, the single worst step, and the team had three competing theories and no evidence. Task: I owned finding the actual cause and recommending a fix within the quarter. Action: I ran a 14-participant moderated study, deliberately recruiting across both verification paths — instant bank-link and manual document upload — and paired it with 30 days of funnel analytics segmented the same way, so qualitative pain points could be checked against where people actually dropped. The diary-style follow-ups showed manual uploaders abandoning when asked to photograph documents on mobile. Result: we moved the manual path behind an instant-link default; drop-off at that step fell from 38% to 22% the next quarter, and because I'd segmented up front, I could show the improvement was concentrated entirely in the cohort that previously hit manual upload — not a general seasonal lift. That segmentation is what let me attribute it.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: That segmentation is what let me attribute it.

expected-yield: surface the limits of the attribution claim — what confounds the segmentation could not rule out, why a 14-person qualitative sample was sufficient to justify a default change, and what tradeoff she accepted by not running a controlled A/B before shipping
